news_summary: Significant strides have been made in advancing the Mine Ban Treaty and protecting children from involvement in armed conflict.
news_summary_fr: Des progrès considérables ont été accomplis dans la promotion du Traité d'interdiction des mines et la protection des enfants contre l'implication dans les conflits armés.
news_summary_es: Se han logrado avances significativos en la promoción del Tratado sobre la Prohibición de las Minas Antipersonal y en la protección de los niños frente a su participación en conflictos armados.

articles.title: Mine Ban Treaty Achieves Progress amid Challenges
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              Deminers search for landmines near Lasinja, a village 40 kilometers south of Zagreb, Croatia, May 29, 2013.
                    © 2013 Sipa via AP Images
          
    


  
Since the 1990s conflicts in the Balkans, Croatia suffered from the scourge of landmines, with hundreds of civilians killed and thousands of acres of land inaccessible due to contamination. In March, the country celebrated becoming mine-free, following a US$1.38 billion, 30-year clearance campaign.Croatia’s story underscores the value of the Mine Ban Treaty and should encourage more countries to join and promote its goals. But on this International Day for Mine Awareness and Assistance in Mine Action, the treaty faces threats from countries withdrawing and from new use of the weapons.Croatia’s heavy contamination stems from 1991-1995, when the main parties to the conflict used landmines. More than 20 percent of the country was contaminated, barring communities from returning and resulting in an annual economic loss of at least $230 million.Interior Minister Davor Božinović said, “This is not just a technical success—it is the fulfillment of a moral obligation to the victims of mines and their families. A mine-free Croatia means safer families, better development of rural areas, more farmland, and stronger tourism.”These successes, a testament to the dedicated work of deminers and sustained government interest, are exactly what the treaty aims to achieve, and the International Day celebrates.Yet, rather than learning from the experiences of Croatia and the more than 30 mine-affected states parties to the treaty that have cleared their land, some countries have abandoned the effort.In the past year, Latvia, Lithuania, Estonia, Finland, and Poland withdrew from the treaty, contending  that antipersonnel landmines were necessary to protect themselves from Russian aggression. In July, Ukraine also sought to unlawfully suspend its obligations under the treaty.New use of antipersonnel landmines in Myanmar, Russia, and Ukraine, as well as along Iran’s borders with Afghanistan and Pakistan, North Korea’s border with South Korea, and the contested Thailand-Cambodia border threatens the norm against these weapons.The International Day for Mine Action affords an opportunity to reflect on the horrific impact of antipersonnel landmines on civilians, the significant improvement in civilians’ lives the treaty has achieved, and the importance of upholding and bolstering opposition to their use. States not party to the treaty should join. All countries should oppose their use and help fund clearance and assistance for victims.
articles.content:
Click to expand Image Deminers search for landmines near Lasinja, a village 40 kilometers south of Zagreb, Croatia, May 29, 2013. © 2013 Sipa via AP Images

Since the 1990s conflicts in the Balkans, Croatia suffered from the scourge of landmines, with hundreds of civilians killed and thousands of acres of land inaccessible due to contamination. In March, the country celebrated becoming mine-free, following a US$1.38 billion, 30-year clearance campaign.

Croatia’s story underscores the value of the Mine Ban Treaty and should encourage more countries to join and promote its goals. But on this International Day for Mine Awareness and Assistance in Mine Action, the treaty faces threats from countries withdrawing and from new use of the weapons.

Croatia’s heavy contamination stems from 1991-1995, when the main parties to the conflict used landmines. More than 20 percent of the country was contaminated, barring communities from returning and resulting in an annual economic loss of at least $230 million.

Interior Minister Davor Božinović said, “This is not just a technical success—it is the fulfillment of a moral obligation to the victims of mines and their families. A mine-free Croatia means safer families, better development of rural areas, more farmland, and stronger tourism.”

These successes, a testament to the dedicated work of deminers and sustained government interest, are exactly what the treaty aims to achieve, and the International Day celebrates.

Yet, rather than learning from the experiences of Croatia and the more than 30 mine-affected states parties to the treaty that have cleared their land, some countries have abandoned the effort.

In the past year, Latvia, Lithuania, Estonia, Finland, and Poland withdrew from the treaty, contending that antipersonnel landmines were necessary to protect themselves from Russian aggression. In July, Ukraine also sought to unlawfully suspend its obligations under the treaty.

New use of antipersonnel landmines in Myanmar, Russia, and Ukraine, as well as along Iran’s borders with Afghanistan and Pakistan, North Korea’s border with South Korea, and the contested Thailand-Cambodia border threatens the norm against these weapons.

The International Day for Mine Action affords an opportunity to reflect on the horrific impact of antipersonnel landmines on civilians, the significant improvement in civilians’ lives the treaty has achieved, and the importance of upholding and bolstering opposition to their use. States not party to the treaty should join. All countries should oppose their use and help fund clearance and assistance for victims.
articles.reasoning: The article reports the significant achievement of Croatia becoming mine-free after a 30-year clearance campaign, which has a broad positive impact on civilian safety, economic development, and rural communities. It highlights the moral and practical benefits of the Mine Ban Treaty, encouraging global adherence despite ongoing challenges. The story is focused, detailed, and underscores a meaningful humanitarian success with tangible benefits for society.
articles.personality_title: Croatia becomes mine-free after 30 years of clearing landmines
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Since the 1990s, Croatia had a serious problem with landmines left from war. These hidden explosives made large areas dangerous and stopped people from using their land. Many civilians were hurt or killed because of these mines.

**What happened** – In March 2024, Croatia announced it is now free of landmines after a 30-year effort to find and remove them. This work cost about $1.38 billion and involved many teams carefully clearing the land.

**Impact** – Becoming mine-free means safer homes for families and better chances for farming and tourism. It also shows how the Mine Ban Treaty, an agreement to stop using landmines, can help countries recover from war. Croatia’s success is a clear example of what the treaty can achieve.

**What's next step** – Despite this progress, some countries have left the treaty and new landmines are still being used in places like Ukraine and Myanmar. The International Day for Mine Awareness reminds the world why stopping landmines matters and encourages more countries to join the treaty and support mine clearance and victim help.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Croatia’s 30-year journey to clear landmines highlights the real benefits of global efforts to ban these weapons and protect communities.
articles.personality_title_fr: La Croatie déminée après 30 ans d'efforts contre les mines terrestres
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis les années 1990, la Croatie était gravement touchée par les mines terrestres laissées après la guerre. Ces explosifs cachés rendaient de grandes zones dangereuses et empêchaient les habitants d’utiliser leurs terres. De nombreux civils ont été blessés ou tués à cause de ces mines.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – En mars 2024, la Croatie a annoncé être désormais exempte de mines terrestres après 30 ans d’efforts pour les trouver et les enlever. Ce travail, qui a coûté environ 1,38 milliard de dollars, a mobilisé de nombreuses équipes pour déminer les terrains.

**Impact** – Être sans mines signifie des maisons plus sûres pour les familles et de meilleures possibilités pour l’agriculture et le tourisme. Cela montre aussi comment le Traité d’interdiction des mines peut aider les pays à se reconstruire après la guerre. Le succès de la Croatie est un exemple clair des résultats possibles.

**Prochaine étape** – Malgré ces progrès, certains pays ont quitté le traité et de nouvelles mines sont toujours utilisées dans des endroits comme l’Ukraine et le Myanmar. La Journée internationale pour la sensibilisation aux mines rappelle au monde l’importance d’arrêter l’utilisation des mines et encourage plus de pays à rejoindre le traité et à soutenir le déminage et l’aide aux victimes.

**Une phrase clé** – Le parcours de 30 ans de la Croatie pour éliminer les mines terrestres montre les bénéfices concrets des efforts mondiaux pour interdire ces armes et protéger les communautés.
articles.personality_title_es: Croacia libre de minas terrestres tras 30 años de desminado
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Desde los años 90, Croacia tuvo un grave problema con minas terrestres dejadas tras la guerra. Estas bombas ocultas hacían peligrosas grandes áreas y impedían que las personas usaran sus tierras. Muchos civiles resultaron heridos o muertos por estas minas.

**Qué pasó** – En marzo de 2024, Croacia anunció que ya está libre de minas terrestres después de 30 años de trabajo para encontrarlas y quitarlas. Esta tarea costó alrededor de 1.380 millones de dólares y contó con muchos equipos que limpiaron cuidadosamente el terreno.

**Impacto** – Estar libre de minas significa hogares más seguros para las familias y mejores oportunidades para la agricultura y el turismo. También muestra cómo el Tratado de Prohibición de Minas puede ayudar a los países a recuperarse después de la guerra. El éxito de Croacia es un claro ejemplo de lo que el tratado puede lograr.

**Próximo paso** – A pesar de este progreso, algunos países han dejado el tratado y todavía se usan minas nuevas en lugares como Ucrania y Myanmar. El Día Internacional para la Concienciación sobre las Minas recuerda al mundo por qué es importante detenerlas y anima a más países a unirse al tratado y apoyar la limpieza y ayuda a las víctimas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los 30 años de Croacia para eliminar minas terrestres muestran los beneficios reales de los esfuerzos globales para prohibir estas armas y proteger a las comunidades.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a peaceful rural Croatian landscape with gently rolling farmland and open fields, where a pair of protective boots stand beside a carefully laid-out metal detector and a cleared, safely marked path winding through the formerly mined land, symbolizing hope, safety, and renewal after decades of landmine clearance.

articles.title: Kids Should Not Be Fighters
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              Children accompany criminal group members in a march in the Delmas neighborhood of Port-au-Prince, Haiti, May 10, 2024.
                    © 2024 Pedro Valtierra Anza/Reuters 
          
    


  
The new campaign of Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps to recruit children as young as 12 for patrols and security checkpoints has been widely condemned. Military recruitment and use of children is a grave violation of children’s rights and a war crime when children are under 15.The world has come a long way in just a few decades. Today, we have treaties prohibiting the conscription or use of child soldiers in armed conflict and courts to hold those responsible to account. Both the International Criminal Court and the Special Court of Sierra Leone have convicted people, from Congolese warlords to the former Liberian President Charles Taylor, for conscripting and using children under age 15 in war. The Child Soldiers Treaty (an optional protocol under the Convention on the Rights of the Child), ratified by 173 countries, prohibits the conscription of children under age 18 or their use in hostilities. Iran has signed although not ratified it.Yet more work is needed. Recruiting and using children ages 15-17 is not consistently recognized as a war crime, nor is recruitment outside of situations formally classified as armed conflicts. This excludes situations like Haiti, where criminal groups recruit children to transport weapons, act as lookouts, and participate in kidnapping and fighting. Some 30 to 50 percent of these criminal group members are under 18. Save the Children says children have been recruited for a pair of sneakers.Countries drafting a new international treaty on crimes against humanity could close that gap. Crimes against humanity are criminal offenses like murder, torture, rape, and slavery committed as part of a widespread or systematic attack on a civilian population. Unlike war crimes, they are crimes during peacetime as well as war.In May 2025, 38 organizations and child rights experts, including Human Rights Watch, endorsed a set of proposals rooted in law and jurisprudence to ensure that a future convention protects children. This month, a new paper from Princeton University’s Liechtenstein Institute on Self-Determination argues that recruiting and using children should be among those crimes.Given global efforts to end the use of child soldiers in war, it shouldn’t be hard to take the next step. Countries have until April 30 to submit proposals to amend the draft treaty. They should use the opportunity to support introducing recruitment and use of children under 18 as a crime against humanity.
articles.content:
Click to expand Image Children accompany criminal group members in a march in the Delmas neighborhood of Port-au-Prince, Haiti, May 10, 2024. © 2024 Pedro Valtierra Anza/Reuters

The new campaign of Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps to recruit children as young as 12 for patrols and security checkpoints has been widely condemned. Military recruitment and use of children is a grave violation of children’s rights and a war crime when children are under 15.

The world has come a long way in just a few decades. Today, we have treaties prohibiting the conscription or use of child soldiers in armed conflict and courts to hold those responsible to account. Both the International Criminal Court and the Special Court of Sierra Leone have convicted people, from Congolese warlords to the former Liberian President Charles Taylor, for conscripting and using children under age 15 in war. The Child Soldiers Treaty (an optional protocol under the Convention on the Rights of the Child), ratified by 173 countries, prohibits the conscription of children under age 18 or their use in hostilities. Iran has signed although not ratified it.

Yet more work is needed. Recruiting and using children ages 15-17 is not consistently recognized as a war crime, nor is recruitment outside of situations formally classified as armed conflicts. This excludes situations like Haiti, where criminal groups recruit children to transport weapons, act as lookouts, and participate in kidnapping and fighting. Some 30 to 50 percent of these criminal group members are under 18. Save the Children says children have been recruited for a pair of sneakers.

Countries drafting a new international treaty on crimes against humanity could close that gap. Crimes against humanity are criminal offenses like murder, torture, rape, and slavery committed as part of a widespread or systematic attack on a civilian population. Unlike war crimes, they are crimes during peacetime as well as war.

In May 2025, 38 organizations and child rights experts, including Human Rights Watch, endorsed a set of proposals rooted in law and jurisprudence to ensure that a future convention protects children. This month, a new paper from Princeton University’s Liechtenstein Institute on Self-Determination argues that recruiting and using children should be among those crimes.

Given global efforts to end the use of child soldiers in war, it shouldn’t be hard to take the next step. Countries have until April 30 to submit proposals to amend the draft treaty. They should use the opportunity to support introducing recruitment and use of children under 18 as a crime against humanity.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights ongoing global efforts and legal progress to end the recruitment and use of child soldiers, a grave human rights violation. It focuses on significant international legal frameworks, the advocacy of numerous organizations, and upcoming treaty amendments that could close critical gaps in protecting children under 18 from recruitment in both war and peacetime. This represents meaningful, broad, and substantive progress toward safeguarding children's rights worldwide.
articles.personality_title: Global push to ban child recruitment expands beyond war zones
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many countries have laws against forcing children under 15 to fight in wars. There are international courts that punish those who break these laws. But children aged 15 to 17, and those recruited outside official wars, like by gangs, are often not protected.

**What happened** – Iran’s Revolutionary Guard Corps recently tried to recruit children as young as 12 for security tasks, drawing strong criticism. In places like Haiti, criminal groups use many children under 18 for dangerous roles. Groups like Human Rights Watch and Save the Children are calling for stronger international rules. A new treaty being drafted could make recruiting children under 18 a crime against humanity, not just a war crime.

**Impact** – This change would protect millions of children worldwide, including those caught in crime-related violence, not just war. It would close loopholes that allow recruiters to escape punishment and recognize that recruiting children is wrong in all violent situations. This is important because it strengthens children’s rights and holds adults responsible for using kids in fights.

**What's next step** – Countries have until April 30 to suggest changes to the treaty. Experts and organizations hope governments will agree to include all child recruitment under crimes against humanity. If successful, this would create stronger global laws to stop the use of child soldiers and protect children in both war and peace.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New international rules could soon make recruiting children under 18 a crime everywhere, closing gaps that leave many children unprotected today.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouvel élan mondial pour interdire le recrutement d’enfants hors zones de guerre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreux pays interdisent de forcer les enfants de moins de 15 ans à combattre dans les guerres. Il existe des tribunaux internationaux pour punir ces crimes. Mais les enfants de 15 à 17 ans, et ceux recrutés en dehors des guerres officielles, comme par des gangs, ne sont souvent pas protégés.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Les Gardiens de la Révolution d’Iran ont récemment tenté de recruter des enfants dès 12 ans pour des tâches de sécurité, ce qui a suscité de vives critiques. En Haïti, des groupes criminels utilisent beaucoup d’enfants de moins de 18 ans pour des rôles dangereux. Des groupes comme Human Rights Watch et Save the Children demandent des règles internationales plus strictes. Un nouveau traité en cours d’élaboration pourrait faire du recrutement d’enfants de moins de 18 ans un crime contre l’humanité, pas seulement un crime de guerre.

**Impact** – Ce changement protégerait des millions d’enfants dans le monde, y compris ceux pris dans la violence liée au crime, pas seulement en temps de guerre. Cela comblerait les failles qui permettent aux recruteurs d’échapper à la justice et reconnaîtrait que recruter des enfants est mauvais dans toutes les situations violentes. C’est important car cela renforce les droits des enfants et tient les adultes responsables.

**Prochaine étape** – Les pays ont jusqu’au 30 avril pour proposer des modifications au traité. Les experts et organisations espèrent que les gouvernements accepteront d’inclure tout recrutement d’enfants dans les crimes contre l’humanité. Si cela réussit, cela créerait des lois mondiales plus fortes pour arrêter l’utilisation des enfants soldats et protéger les enfants en temps de guerre comme de paix.

**Phrase clé** – De nouvelles règles internationales pourraient bientôt faire du recrutement d’enfants de moins de 18 ans un crime partout, comblant les lacunes qui laissent beaucoup d’enfants sans protection aujourd’hui.
articles.personality_title_es: Impulso global para prohibir el reclutamiento de niños más allá de zonas de guerra
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchos países tienen leyes que prohíben obligar a niños menores de 15 años a luchar en guerras. Hay tribunales internacionales que castigan estos delitos. Pero los niños de 15 a 17 años, y los reclutados fuera de guerras oficiales, como por pandillas, a menudo no están protegidos.

**Qué pasó** – La Guardia Revolucionaria de Irán intentó reclutar niños desde los 12 años para tareas de seguridad, lo que generó críticas fuertes. En lugares como Haití, grupos criminales usan a muchos niños menores de 18 años para roles peligrosos. Organizaciones como Human Rights Watch y Save the Children piden reglas internacionales más fuertes. Un nuevo tratado en preparación podría convertir el reclutamiento de niños menores de 18 años en un crimen de lesa humanidad, no solo un crimen de guerra.

**Impacto** – Este cambio protegería a millones de niños en todo el mundo, incluyendo a los atrapados en violencia criminal, no solo en guerra. Cerraría vacíos legales que permiten a reclutadores evitar castigos y reconocería que reclutar niños es incorrecto en todas las situaciones violentas. Esto es importante porque fortalece los derechos de los niños y responsabiliza a los adultos.

**Próximo paso** – Los países tienen hasta el 30 de abril para proponer cambios al tratado. Expertos y organizaciones esperan que los gobiernos apoyen incluir todo reclutamiento infantil como crimen de lesa humanidad. Si tiene éxito, crearía leyes globales más fuertes para detener el uso de niños soldados y proteger a los niños en guerra y en paz.

**Frase clave** – Nuevas reglas internacionales podrían pronto hacer que reclutar niños menores de 18 años sea un crimen en todas partes, cerrando vacíos que hoy dejan a muchos niños sin protección.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a group of small, empty children’s shoes arranged in a protective circle around a glowing, unarmed white dove perched on a broken rifle lying peacefully on the ground, set against a soft, natural background of muted earth tones.
